transcript: Look again, European Paintings 1300-1800, a suite of 45 galleries with over 700 works of art, is an opportunity to see our collection in a new light. Europe is an idea in flux, a geography in flux. There have been moments of expansion, empire, division, and cultural exchange. We wanted to think about our galleries as not only a progression of style unfolding, but as a network of ideas. It really is an invitation to look again, to think again, to have new favorite paintings, to grapple with sometimes difficult histories, and to visit over and over and each time encounter something new. We've organized the galleries chronologically that will give our visitors the basic spine or backbone in which to walk through a history of art. But within that chronology, you'll find moments where we spread out, either geographically or around a given genre of painting, to ask questions about that history like what are the limits of europe what are its borders as traditionally defined or as we know through histories of imperialism colonialism that in effect span european culture around the globe for visitors who have spent much time with our collection it's a moment to see highlights real destination, but it's also an opportunity to discover lesser-known works that fill out the larger social and historical as well as creative context of European painting. This project was prompted by a physical need to replace the skylights, but we also have undertaken a series of architectural interventions, including moving many doorways and establishing new sight lines that we hope will guide the visitors through what is frankly an enormous suite of galleries. To rehang the collection was a great opportunity as well to look outside the department's holdings to other curatorial departments at the Met, as well as even to paintings that were made beyond the chronological limits and geographical boundaries of these galleries. All of this to really underscore that there isn't one singular way of looking at the history of European painting. There are many histories, and to invite visitors to consider that. We wanted to begin our story by giving visitors some orientation to Europe, both as a geography, but also as a concept, a territory whose borders have shifted over the centuries and are still contested to this day. One of the things that has been mobilized in the past to unite Europe is the idea of a shared heritage of classical antiquity from the Mediterranean. Our opening gallery with the very grand and monumental works by the Venetian painter Tiepolo also includes a number of loans from other curatorial departments. Those Tiepolo paintings depict ancient Roman history. And by placing them in dialogue with other works, we wanted to think about the very expansive, very complicated and global legacy of Mediterranean antiquity. It's not just something that belongs to what is now recognized as Europe. This is a tradition that has been responded to across the world, and that ranges from the sculpture of a bodhisattva we have from present-day Pakistan to an 18th century work by a Mexican indigenous artist that depicts a story told by the ancient Roman poet Virgil. This is a batea, a type of shallow wooden tray. It is the work of an artist named José Manuel de la Cerda, an indigenous artist who worked in the city of Pátzcuaro in Michoacán in western Mexico in the middle of the 18th century. It would have been displayed I don't know. artist who worked in the city of Pátzcuaro in Michoacán in western Mexico in the middle of the 18th century. It would have been displayed along with 11 other bateas from the same series devoted to Virgil's Aeneid in the Viceroyal Palace in Mexico City. Starting here, it says Aeneas provoca a la guerra turno, so turnos, provoked into war by Annias, which leads to the founding of Rome. This is a narrative that was adopted by the Spanish monarchy and many other European monarchies as a legitimizing myth. It was also used to legitimize the Spanish conquest of mexico so the whole narrative of crossing the atlantic was compared to aeneas's voyage so it's a really really complex object and i like to think that it speaks to the theme of this introductory gallery in that it causes us not only to look again, but to think again about what Europe is historically, culturally, and even geographically. This gallery is interesting for a paintings gallery because there isn't a single painting on the wall. We look at works that stood on altarpieces, small devotional images. Paintings were held in one's hands. They often were companions on travel, and people returned to them time and again in different contexts and different conditions. Here we're looking at an extraordinary work by Duccio, one of the earliest in our collection, a work made by a Sienese artist around 1300 looking to Byzantine icons from the east as its source. This remarkable painting was made at a moment where Italian artists are really coming to terms We'll be a painter. The image of the Virgin Mary was a fairly prescriptive image. It was established by legend when the Virgin appeared to Saint Luke and said, here I am, capture my likeness. What I love about this painting is it shows a mother and her child in a kind of tender connection and trying to capture that spirit in paint. It really is a spark to recognizing how artists at the start of our story are looking at their own place in relation to a long history of image making. We've placed Duccio's Madonna next to a devotional image by the French 19th century painter Ingres. Not only does this bracket the chronological span of what we're looking at, but I love the mirror of a work at the beginning of our story next to a work made 500 years later. When we enter this gallery, we encounter a range of faces from 15th century Europe. Before this moment, portraiture was primarily reserved for the aristocracy or leaders of the church. And what we have here is a rising upper middle class who have new capabilities for commissioning art and a growing interest in having their individual likenesses captured for purposes of commemoration, projections of social status, but also for devotional reasons. These are portraits painted by the Bruges artist Hans Memling, and they depict Tommaso and Maria Portinari. Tommaso was the branch manager of the Medici Bank in Bruges, and they were one of many Italian clients who sought out Hans Memling because of the real balance he strikes between idealization and individualization. Memling specialized in details of the physiognomy, like small wrinkles around the eyes or even a scar on Tommaso's chin. We have his five o'clock shadow. Just as much attention has been given to their sumptuous costumes, which are really projecting their social status. There's such a sense of aliveness and individuality with these sitters that it can kind of trick us into thinking we actually have a better sense of who they were than maybe we really do. This work was painted in 1470, likely to commemorate the marriage of the couple. What we don't really have a sense of here is the marked age difference between Tommaso and Maria. He was 42 at the time they married and she was only 14. So that real age difference and power imbalance from the perspective of our current time and culture is not reflected. We have much more of a sense of them as equals. Maria has such independence and self-possession here. This gallery looks at an international style in Europe from around 1350 to the first decades of the 15th century. We call it international Gothic, a kind of court style that was a brand almost throughout the continent. This is a style that draws as much from sculpture as it does from metalwork, from jewels, from illuminated manuscripts, from religious reliquaries. It's wonderful to think of an artist in northern Italy or an artist in Prague or England, all thinking about certain questions or even looking at similar objects to work in their respective cultural contexts. And it's wonderful to see the different layers even in a small painting, such as this Virgin and Child enthroned by a painter active in Prague in the middle of the 14th century. This figure of the Virgin, so courtly, so refined, so elegant, embodies all of the best qualities of Gothic illumination. But the structure in which she's placed reveals an artist that understands the sophisticated engagement with perspectival space that the Italian artists were trying to define in the 14th century. The rediscovery of Rome and antiquity in the 15th and early 16th centuries in Italy is such an important moment to consider. Rome was not only an important idea, but it was also an important reality. Its monuments could be looked at with a distance, and a recognition of that distance allowed for an extraordinary exploration. One thing I love about this gallery is that it brings home in the paintings and in the sculptures how ancient Rome was not just quoted, but actually lived by the Venetians at this moment. And perhaps the figure in this gallery that expresses this point is Venus herself. And we're looking at the Venus Marina, the Venus who was born of the sea. We can identify her as Venus born of the sea because her attribute is the dolphin. And maps of Venice at the period point out that the dolphin is the emblem of the city. But what is compelling about this work is how her kind of mellifluous form, the way that the light picks up and shines off of the details of her flesh, is so much akin to the magnificent paintings that are nearby. It really does allow us to consider the effects of light and form the same way that the artists are trying to capture that in two dimension on the flat surfaces of these canvases. I also love when we look at this picture between the Venus and then the warrior statuette. We see that there is this constant tension in Venice between the arts of peace and the arts of war symbolized by Venus and by Mars. There's this concept consistent in antiquity that peace is only possible through martial readiness. It's extraordinary to think of Venice known as well from making bronze cannons would also use those same furnaces to make such remarkable works of art. The focus of this thematic gallery is the emergence of landscape as an independent genre from the late 15th century to the early 17th century. So as you know, prior to this point, landscapes are really more seen as backdrops to religious subject matter. But here, landscape is starting to emerge as its own protagonist. I think we really see that in the Patinir. We have this just absolutely dazzling vista into the distance. For me, the thing that immediately comes to mind is the vantage point that the artist has taken. He's offering us a true bird's eye view of a landscape. And that's something that changes by and large as time goes on into the later 16th and 17th centuries, where artists tend to favor a much lower viewpoint, as if the artist is on the ground and we as viewers are also on the ground, almost like we could just step right into the landscape. Here we have one of the iconic landscapes of our collection, Peter Bruegel the Elder's Harvesters from the 16th century. This is a work that even if we still have a bit of a bird's eye view, you feel like you can step into it. Of course, this is a kind of watershed in the history of European landscape and the fact that a work of this size, no less, would just show, at least on the surface, a purely quotidian scene. Right. Unlike the patinier, there's no explicit religious subject matter. There's this tension, right? Because what the artist is explicitly showing us is a landscape that is shaped by human labor, even we could say engineering. So the geometric forms of the cut fields of wheat and the bales. When we think about this painting as one made for a particular elite urban viewer, I think it helps us recognize that we're also seeing a social structuring here, right? The very fact that the peasants are depicted for the pleasure of this Antwerp merchant and banker who benefited from their labor itself is a reminder that this isn't necessarily an accurate depiction of what this life was really like. here we have a hyper local focus on later 15th and 16th century painting in three cities in what is now present-day Belgium, Bruges, Antwerp, and Brussels. Due to the prosperity and the independence of these very cosmopolitan centers, we see for the first time in Europe the emergence of a year-round art market. The art market, with its competitive environment, is changing the way painters are working in their workshops, but it's also changing the subjects that they choose to depict. In terms of process, we see different painters developing particular specializations and then teaming up to collaborate on a particular work to produce bespoke objects combining the artistic strengths of both makers. A great example of that is this wonderful triptych that's a collaboration between the Antwerp painter Jos van Cleve who specialized in painting figures and an artist whose name we don't know today, but who was equally skillful and specialized in landscapes. In terms of changes to the subjects that were depicted, what we see more and more is a foregrounding of elements that are not strictly obligatory for the religious subjects, be it landscape or still life or domestic elements, so-called genre elements taken from quotidian life. Here we have one panel of an altarpiece by the artist Bernard Van Orley, who was based in Brussels. It depicts the life of St. John the Baptist. What I love about this birth scene is that, first of all, we have it set in what essentially is a sumptuous, courtly 16th century interior. And we're really given this view of women at work caring for the new baby and the mother. So this kind of combination of humble details and also courtly elements. You'll have seen from the other galleries that we've put a real emphasis on looking across Europe, sometimes even across the globe. But sometimes where our holdings in a single artist are so strong, we've devoted entire galleries to one towering figure in the history of European painting. That's certainly the case with Rembrandt. We have one of the greatest collections of his works in the world, and we're particularly fortunate in being able to represent almost all facets of his long career. I'd say the crowning jewel of our collection of Rembrandt is Aristotle contemplating a bust of Homer, and this is a painting that you know intimately, having conserved it for over a year. Yes, it was a privilege to work on this picture and as you know it was last cleaned in 1980, 40 years ago. The problem that developed on the picture is that the varnish had deteriorated and it had lost its clarity and with an artist like Rembrandt it's so important to maintain that golden glow. He has a very particular way of painting and uses a very unconventional combination of materials. But as a result of this, there are certain chemical changes that take place. We now have much more understanding of these chemical changes. And so I was able to use methods to remove the salt deposits which were causing the appearance of degradation. I'll never forget coming up pretty early in the treatment, and suddenly that pile of books that is in the left-hand corner registered to me. It became legible. In a way, I have to say, even being the curator of this painting, I'd never contemplated that detail before, but you absolutely brought it back for us. And I think that's true of so many individual passages of this picture. He's done something wonderful with his paint where he actually builds up the areas of highlighting so that they become almost sculptural. And at the same time, he conveys an amazing sense of interiority we feel that we're seeing a real person lost in thought it's almost as though Rembrandt has conjured up an act of thinking right before our eyes it's such a privileged thing to actually work over the surface of a picture and see every nuance of the painting. And as you're uncovering, it's always the most marvelous thing to see when every single brushstroke had an intelligence to it, which is meaningful. Ogreko is a key figure in the history of art, but also stands a little outside of typical stylistic developments. He's someone who comes from Greece. He trains in Italy, looking particularly at Italian Renaissance painters, and then takes off in Spain, where he actually has an incredibly successful career. But then after his death, he sort of goes silent for almost two centuries, in which his work is seen as somehow somehow outside the norm and certainly as a neoclassical moment takes hold in europe and so the result is you have someone who's lost for a long period of time and then comes back with a vengeance when he's rediscovered at the end of the 19th century this is a greco's view of the city of toledo it's a painting he painted around the year 1600, and this is an imaginary view, actually, of the city that's charged with a kind of emotional intensity that a more topographical view might have lacked. One of the most incredible things about this painting is the way that El Greco engages the full range of the surface of the painting itself in this kind of faceted effect and ricochet of light and shade. The painting next to it is a work by Paul Cézanne. It's a work from the 1890s. Cézanne was really interested in El Greco from the 1880s, a period during which he's actually copying the work of El Greco that he can see in collections in Paris. I think this is really an opportunity to try to see through the artist's eye what was it that Cézanne was seeing there. Of course, it's about context. It's about the actual link of Cézanne standing in a room in front of a painting, and that's all historically documented. But what, in terms of kind of indescribable visual characteristics, links these things is something that can only happen in the gallery, in sort of lived space by the visitor and their emotional reaction to those two paintings hanging next to each other. So our galleries are really rethinking Spanish painting of the so-called Golden Age. One of the ways we're doing that is to think of Spain in its capacity as a remarkably multiracial society in the 17th century, also one that is connected globally. The portrait of Wanda Pereja by Diego Velazquez represents a man of African descent who was enslaved by Velazquez for over 20 years in his studio before Pereja, the man you see in this painting, became a painter in his own right at the end of his life. What attracts me about the portrait is not just the familiar way in which Velázquez had portrayed his assistant, but also the way in which by being an assistant to Velázquez, Juan de Pereja would have been in charge of stretching the canvas and applying those preparatory layers. And so clearly he had a contribution in the making of his own portrait. One of the things we really were trying to lean into with the re-installation was to ask how the introduction of things that had not traditionally been in these galleries allow us to rethink what a European tradition might even mean. This is the first time that the museum is showing our works from the Viceroyalty of New Spain, New Granada, and Peru together in one room, which really gives us the opportunity to have wider conversations about their relationship to European art and their differences with these European canons. It also allows us to have a conversation of the development of the art of painting as a global phenomenon rather than a European one. Perhaps one of the most dazzling pictures in this gallery is this representation of the Virgin Balfonera from the Cusco School in Peru, dated to the 1770s. It's quite interesting in the use of an iconography that comes from Spain by a community of people from La Rioja Province to venerate images of their local virgins. One of the leitmotifs that you see in Cusco school is the use of brocateado, this brocade-type technique in which gold leaf is applied onto textiles that could be in order to represent dress statues or things like polychrome statues with graffito motifs. It's about texture and this idea of representation more so than accuracy that you would think in the term of perspective. That's what's fascinating is to identify geographies and periods in time in which the assumed goals or priorities are not necessarily what they were in, say, Paris or Rome or Madrid. And that's such a refreshing way to look at the history of painting. One of my areas of curatorial responsibility at the Met is 18th century British painting, and I was excited with this gallery to have the opportunity to complicate the geography of what we consider 18th century British art. We've included a pair of very generous loans from our colleagues in the American wing that are paintings made by artists who are now considered founding figures in the history of American art, but who were actually working in London at the time, which was the destination for ambitious painters from North America at the end of the 18th century. As many times as I've seen this painting, I never can get over how wonderful the cocked elbow is. That's a motif that goes right back to the high Renaissance. Copley has clearly studied it, and also its different permutations in the work of artists working in England like Van Dyck, like Reynolds. It conveys so much swagger, so much confidence, but at the same time, he's so young, he's so fragile-looking in many ways. He's about to embark on this very dangerous career as a midshipman in the British Navy. And I think that Copley has so skillfully captured that mixture of youth, but also courage. And also, I think it's important to say, he a child of great privilege you know he's serving on the ship of his father and he grows up to become a rear admiral as well but i agree with you there's just that wonderful sensitivity and of course copley is seen as the most important painter working in the british american colonies because he can capture this intensity of character personality there's a really living quality to his figures on canvas. But also what this character, this young boy, conveys in terms of it being a product of empire in a sense. This massive history painting by John Trumbull gives our presentation of 18th century British and Anglo-American painting a real jolt, I think. It's an image of imperial warfare, an image of death. It's an image that celebrates the heroism, the nobility of British soldiers, and by extension, the British Empire. And I think that that context becomes a little more clear when it's shown surrounded by other British as opposed to American paintings. And what Trumbull has chosen to focus on, certainly the drama of the fight, the battle, the incredible sky, even the Rock of Gibraltar, you know, is illuminated by those flares. And the focus on this very poignant death of this Spanish officer who is resisting the assistance offered by the British. So this homage to, of course, the pain of war, but also the nobleness of the cause, even though they're on different sides. And I think it underlies the point that we're talking about colonial societies, right? I mean, so this is the British American, Anglo-American narrative in art history and geopolitics. And I think when you see this in dialogue with the often very placid or tranquil grand portraits of British aristocrats that we also have on display, it makes clear some of the historical context that was undergirding the flowering of British culture and power in the 18th century. One way to think about the last sequence of rooms in the European Paintings Galleries is to understand the rise of France in particular, and also within France, the Salon Exhibition. This is an annual exhibition put on by the Royal Academy that really dominates not only French painting, but by the mid-18th century dominates European painting more generally. This gallery in particular looks at the 18th century when the Salon as an exhibition space and the French Academy really structured artists' lives almost completely, and eventually the public exhibition of works of art. Artists are painting specifically for exhibition, not necessarily even with a patron. This is a new phenomenon. It's not painting for a church, it's not painting for a given collector, but rather for publicity and the critics. It becomes all of the groundwork for thinking about the 19th and to the 20th century, but also for how we view art today, even the phenomenon of art museums, the idea that works are there for your enjoyment as a public, the idea of a public art viewing being part of urban culture. This is Adelaide Labiguillard's self-portrait, a monumental work that was shown at the Salon of 1785, and she depicts herself before the easel with two of her pupils. And it's really seen as a kind of manifesto of women's art education and role as professional painters, which she shows with great accomplishment and gusto here in this ambitious self-portrait that includes herself dressed really to the nines and this is very typical of artists in this moment as they tried to establish themselves as respectable as a profession that was akin to the liberal arts rather than just a kind of manual activity they often showed themselves as people who'd achieved a certain in the world. And clearly between the furnishings and her dress, her hat, all of these things are making that clear. With the French Revolution, the number of women who would participate in public exhibitions exploded. And the Met is really fortunate in that we have an extremely strong core group of works by women painters from the 1770s onward. But this kind of image shows you the public visibility of women as painters at the end of the Ancien Regime. One goal that we had for our reinstallation was to show off the many recent acquisitions we've made that allow us to tell an expanded narrative of European painting. And one of my favorites among these recent acquisitions is this fantastic portrait of the Dutch artist, Rahel Rous, that Rous painted as a collaboration with another artist, Michiel van Musser. We actually know about the collaboration, not just because the two hands are visible, but because there's a poetic inscription on this opened album of floral studies. And it roughly translates along the lines of, Behold how Rouse made her brush shine when Musser painted her from life. And it's a fascinating moment of a kind of gentle competition, rivalry, perhaps between the still life painter and the portraitist. But what the poem makes clear is that Rouse was the much more famous painter at the time. And I think it should open our eyes to the celebrity of a woman artist and also the prominence of the genre of still life painting in the late 17th century. Still life has often been dismissed as somehow lesser than history painting, than scenes from myth or the Bible. But we see here a consummate artist at work in her studio, having tribute paid to her by her colleagues and friends. We wanted to put this work on view initially to think again about a subject matter that has been a constant in European painting, and that is paintings that reflect on the identity of the artist, of what it means to be a painter, and how does art get made, what is the space of the studio. One thing that will be striking to visitors to this particular gallery is that a lot of the work expands the geography and the chronology of our department. We have self-portraits by 20th century artists, and by including the work by Kerry James Marshall, we have a major 21st century allegory of art making. All of these things are to emphasize that this tradition of investigating the conditions of art making is something that is very much going on today. We hope visitors will approach our galleries with a sense of creativity, a sense of possibility, and a sense that they can make connections across our 45 galleries and return to these dialogues again and again. We think of these galleries as experimental spaces, as playful spaces, places where you can tell multiple and sometimes conflicting stories. We wanted to signal that we are open to discussing new questions and themes, issues, and concerns that traditionally haven't been addressed in this space. To explore the nuances, the more subtle questions that allow visitors to get a much broader sense of what European paintings are and how we might consider them today.

The rediscovery of Rome and antiquity in the 15th and early 16th centuries in Italy is such an important moment to consider. Rome was not only an important idea, but it was also an important reality. Its monuments could be looked at with a distance, and a recognition of that distance allowed for an extraordinary exploration. One thing I love about this gallery is that it brings home in the paintings and in the sculptures how ancient Rome was not just quoted, but actually lived by the Venetians at this moment. And perhaps the figure in this gallery that expresses this point is Venus herself. And we're looking at the Venus Marina, the Venus who was born of the sea. We can identify her as Venus born of the sea because her attribute is the dolphin. And maps of Venice at the period point out that the dolphin is the emblem of the city. But what is compelling about this work is how her kind of mellifluous form, the way that the light picks up and shines off of the details of her flesh, is so much akin to the magnificent paintings that are nearby. It really does allow us to consider the effects of light and form the same way that the artists are trying to capture that in two dimension on the flat surfaces of these canvases. I also love when we look at this picture between the Venus and then the warrior statuette. We see that there is this constant tension in Venice between the arts of peace and the arts of war symbolized by Venus and by Mars. There's this concept consistent in antiquity that peace is only possible through martial readiness. It's extraordinary to think of Venice known as well from making bronze cannons would also use those same furnaces to make such remarkable works of art. The focus of this thematic gallery is the emergence of landscape as an independent genre from the late 15th century to the early 17th century. So as you know, prior to this point, landscapes are really more seen as backdrops to religious subject matter. But here, landscape is starting to emerge as its own protagonist. I think we really see that in the Patinir. We have this just absolutely dazzling vista into the distance. For me, the thing that immediately comes to mind is the vantage point that the artist has taken. He's offering us a true bird's eye view of a landscape. And that's something that changes by and large as time goes on into the later 16th and 17th centuries, where artists tend to favor a much lower viewpoint, as if the artist is on the ground and we as viewers are also on the ground, almost like we could just step right into the landscape. Here we have one of the iconic landscapes of our collection, Peter Bruegel the Elder's Harvesters from the 16th century. This is a work that even if we still have a bit of a bird's eye view, you feel like you can step into it. Of course, this is a kind of watershed in the history of European landscape and the fact that a work of this size, no less, would just show, at least on the surface, a purely quotidian scene. Right. Unlike the patinier, there's no explicit religious subject matter. There's this tension, right? Because what the artist is explicitly showing us is a landscape that is shaped by human labor, even we could say engineering. So the geometric forms of the cut fields of wheat and the bales. When we think about this painting as one made for a particular elite urban viewer, I think it helps us recognize that we're also seeing a social structuring here, right? The very fact that the peasants are depicted for the pleasure of this Antwerp merchant and banker who benefited
from their labor itself is a reminder that this isn't necessarily an accurate depiction

of what this life was really like. here we have a hyper local focus on later 15th and 16th century painting in three cities in what is now present-day Belgium, Bruges, Antwerp, and Brussels. Due to the prosperity and the independence of these very cosmopolitan centers, we see for the first time in Europe the emergence of a year-round art market. The art market, with its competitive environment, is changing the way painters are working in their workshops, but it's also changing the subjects that they choose to depict. In terms of process, we see different painters developing particular specializations and then teaming up to collaborate on a particular work to produce bespoke objects combining the artistic strengths of both makers. A great example of that is this wonderful triptych that's a collaboration between the Antwerp painter Jos van Cleve who specialized in painting figures and an artist whose name we don't know today, but who was equally skillful and specialized in landscapes. In terms of changes to the subjects that were depicted, what we see more and more is a foregrounding of elements that are not strictly obligatory for the religious subjects, be it landscape or still life or domestic elements, so-called genre elements taken from quotidian life. Here we have one panel of an altarpiece by the artist Bernard Van Orley, who was based in Brussels. It depicts the life of St. John the Baptist. What I love about this birth scene is that, first of all, we have it set in what essentially is a sumptuous, courtly 16th century interior. And we're really given this view of women at work caring for the new baby and the mother. So this kind of combination of humble details and also courtly elements. You'll have seen from the other galleries that we've put a real emphasis on looking across Europe, sometimes even across the globe. But sometimes where our holdings in a single artist are so strong, we've devoted entire galleries to one towering figure in the history of European painting. That's certainly the case with Rembrandt. We have one of the greatest collections of his works in the world, and we're particularly fortunate in being able to represent almost all facets of his long career. I'd say the crowning jewel of our collection of Rembrandt is Aristotle contemplating a bust of Homer, and this is a painting that you know intimately, having conserved it for over a year. Yes, it was a privilege to work on this picture and as you know it was last cleaned in 1980, 40 years ago. The problem that developed on the picture is that the varnish had deteriorated and it had lost its clarity and with an artist like Rembrandt it's so important to maintain that golden glow. He has a very particular way of painting and uses a very unconventional combination of materials. But as a result of this, there are certain chemical changes that take place. We now have much more understanding of these chemical changes. And so I was able to use methods to remove the salt deposits which were causing the appearance of degradation. I'll never forget coming up pretty early in the treatment, and suddenly that pile of books that is in the left-hand corner registered to me. It became legible. In a way, I have to say, even being the curator of this painting, I'd never contemplated that detail before, but you absolutely brought it back for us. And I think that's true of so many individual passages of this picture. He's done something wonderful with his paint where he actually builds up the areas of highlighting so that they become almost sculptural. And at the same time, he conveys an amazing sense of interiority we feel that we're seeing a real person lost in thought it's almost as though Rembrandt has conjured up an act of thinking right before our eyes it's such a privileged thing to actually work over the surface of a picture and see every nuance of the painting.
And as you're uncovering,
it's always the most marvelous thing
to see when every single brushstroke
had an intelligence to it, which is meaningful.
Ogreko is a key figure in the history of art,
but also stands a little outside
of typical stylistic developments.

He's someone who comes from Greece. He trains in Italy, looking particularly at Italian Renaissance painters, and then takes off in Spain, where he actually has an incredibly successful career. But then after his death, he sort of goes silent for almost two centuries, in which his work is seen as somehow somehow outside the norm and certainly as a neoclassical moment takes hold in europe and so the result is you have someone who's lost for a long period of time and then comes back with a vengeance when he's rediscovered at the end of the 19th century this is a greco's view of the city of toledo it's a painting he painted around the year 1600, and this is an imaginary view, actually, of the city that's charged with a kind of emotional intensity that a more topographical view might have lacked. One of the most incredible things about this painting is the way that El Greco engages the full range of the surface of the painting itself in this kind of faceted effect and ricochet of light and shade. The painting next to it is a work by Paul Cézanne. It's a work from the 1890s. Cézanne was really interested in El Greco from the 1880s, a period during which he's actually copying the work of El Greco that he can see in collections in Paris. I think this is really an opportunity to try to see through the artist's eye what was it that Cézanne was seeing there. Of course, it's about context. It's about the actual link of Cézanne standing in a room in front of a painting, and that's all historically documented. But what, in terms of kind of indescribable visual characteristics, links these things is something that can only happen in the gallery, in sort of lived space by the visitor and their emotional reaction to those two paintings hanging next to each other. So our galleries are really rethinking Spanish painting of the so-called Golden Age. One of the ways we're doing that is to think of Spain in its capacity as a remarkably multiracial society in the 17th century, also one that is connected globally. The portrait of Wanda Pereja by Diego Velazquez represents a man of African descent who was enslaved by Velazquez for over 20 years in his studio before Pereja, the man you see in this painting, became a painter in his own right at the end of his life. What attracts me about the portrait is not just the familiar way in which Velázquez had portrayed his assistant, but also the way in which by being an assistant to Velázquez, Juan de Pereja would have been in charge of stretching the canvas and applying those preparatory layers. And so clearly he had a contribution in the making of his own portrait. One of the things we really were trying to lean into with the re-installation was to ask how the introduction of things that had not traditionally been in these galleries allow us to rethink what a European tradition might even mean. This is the first time that the museum is showing our works from the Viceroyalty of New Spain, New Granada, and Peru together in one room, which really gives us the opportunity to have wider conversations about their relationship to European art and their differences with these European canons. It also allows us to have a conversation of the development of the art of painting as a global phenomenon rather than a European one. Perhaps one of the most dazzling pictures in this gallery is this representation of the Virgin Balfonera from the Cusco School in Peru, dated to the 1770s. It's quite interesting in the use of an iconography that comes from Spain by a community of people from La Rioja Province to venerate images of their local virgins. One of the leitmotifs that you see in Cusco school is the use of brocateado, this brocade-type technique in which gold leaf is applied onto textiles that could be in order to represent dress statues or things like polychrome statues with graffito motifs. It's about texture and this idea of representation
more so than accuracy that you would think in the term of perspective.
That's what's fascinating is to identify geographies and periods in time
in which the assumed goals or priorities
are not necessarily what they were in, say, Paris or Rome or Madrid.

And that's such a refreshing way to look at the history of painting. One of my areas of curatorial responsibility at the Met is 18th century British painting, and I was excited with this gallery to have the opportunity to complicate the geography of what we consider 18th century British art. We've included a pair of very generous loans from our colleagues in the American wing that are paintings made by artists who are now considered founding figures in the history of American art, but who were actually working in London at the time, which was the destination for ambitious painters from North America at the end of the 18th century. As many times as I've seen this painting, I never can get over how wonderful the cocked elbow is. That's a motif that goes right back to the high Renaissance. Copley has clearly studied it, and also its different permutations in the work of artists working in England like Van Dyck, like Reynolds. It conveys so much swagger, so much confidence, but at the same time, he's so young, he's so fragile-looking in many ways. He's about to embark on this very dangerous career as a midshipman in the British Navy. And I think that Copley has so skillfully captured that mixture of youth, but also courage. And also, I think it's important to say, he a child of great privilege you know he's serving on the ship of his father and he grows up to become a rear admiral as well but i agree with you there's just that wonderful sensitivity and of course copley is seen as the most important painter working in the british american colonies because he can capture this intensity of character personality there's a really living quality to his figures on canvas. But also what this character, this young boy, conveys in terms of it being a product of empire in a sense. This massive history painting by John Trumbull gives our presentation of 18th century British and Anglo-American painting a real jolt, I think. It's an image of imperial warfare, an image of death. It's an image that celebrates the heroism, the nobility of British soldiers, and by extension, the British Empire. And I think that that context becomes a little more clear when it's shown surrounded by other British as opposed to American paintings. And what Trumbull has chosen to focus on, certainly the drama of the fight, the battle, the incredible sky, even the Rock of Gibraltar, you know, is illuminated by those flares. And the focus on this very poignant death of this Spanish officer who is resisting the assistance offered by the British. So this homage to, of course, the pain of war, but also the nobleness of the cause, even though they're on different sides. And I think it underlies the point that we're talking about colonial societies, right? I mean, so this is the British American, Anglo-American narrative in art history and geopolitics. And I think when you see this in dialogue with the often very placid or tranquil grand portraits of British aristocrats that we also have on display, it makes clear some of the historical context that was undergirding the flowering of British culture and power in the 18th century. One way to think about the last sequence of rooms in the European Paintings Galleries is to understand the rise of France in particular, and also within France, the Salon Exhibition. This is an annual exhibition put on by the Royal Academy that really dominates not only French painting, but by the mid-18th century dominates European painting more generally. This gallery in particular looks at the 18th century when the Salon as an exhibition space and the French Academy really structured artists' lives almost completely, and eventually the public exhibition of works of art. Artists are painting specifically for exhibition, not necessarily even with a patron. This is a new phenomenon. It's not painting for a church, it's not painting for a given collector, but rather for publicity and the critics. It becomes all of the groundwork for thinking about the 19th and to the 20th century, but also for how we view art today, even the phenomenon of art museums, the idea that works are there for your enjoyment as a public, the idea of a public art viewing being part of urban culture. This is Adelaide Labiguillard's self-portrait, a monumental work that was shown at the Salon of 1785, and she depicts herself before the easel with two of her pupils. And it's really seen as a kind of manifesto of women's art education and role as professional painters, which she shows with great accomplishment and gusto here in this ambitious self-portrait that includes herself dressed really to the nines and this is very typical of artists in this moment as they tried to establish themselves as respectable as a profession that was akin to the liberal arts rather than just a kind of manual activity they often showed themselves as people who'd achieved a certain in the world. And clearly between the furnishings and her dress, her hat, all of these things are making that clear. With the French Revolution, the number of women who would participate in public exhibitions exploded. And the Met is really fortunate in that we have an extremely strong core group of works by women painters from the 1770s onward. But this kind of image shows you the public visibility of women as painters at the end of the Ancien Regime. One goal that we had for our reinstallation was to show off the many recent acquisitions we've made that allow us to tell an expanded narrative of European painting. And one of my favorites among these recent acquisitions is this fantastic portrait of the Dutch artist, Rahel Rous, that Rous painted as a collaboration with another artist, Michiel van Musser. We actually know about the collaboration, not just because the two hands are visible, but because there's a poetic inscription on this opened album of floral studies. And it roughly translates along the lines of, Behold how Rouse made her brush shine when Musser painted her from life. And it's a fascinating moment of a kind of gentle competition, rivalry, perhaps between the still life painter and the portraitist. But what the poem makes clear is that Rouse was the much more famous painter at the time. And I think it should open our eyes to the celebrity of a woman artist and also the prominence of the genre of still life painting in the late 17th century. Still life has often been dismissed as somehow lesser than history painting, than scenes from myth or the Bible. But we see here a consummate artist at work in her studio, having tribute paid to her by her colleagues and friends. We wanted to put this work on view initially to think again about a subject matter that has been a constant in European painting, and that is paintings that reflect on the identity of the artist, of what it means to be a painter, and how does art get made, what is the space of the studio. One thing that will be striking to visitors to this particular gallery is that a lot of the work expands the geography and the chronology of our department. We have self-portraits by 20th century artists, and by including the work by Kerry James Marshall, we have a major 21st century allegory of art making. All of these things are to emphasize that this tradition of investigating the conditions of art making is something that is very much going on today. We hope visitors will approach our galleries with a sense of creativity, a sense of possibility, and a sense that they can make connections across our 45 galleries and return to these dialogues again and again. We think of these galleries as experimental spaces, as playful spaces, places where you can tell multiple and sometimes conflicting stories. We wanted to signal that we are open to discussing new questions and themes, issues, and concerns that traditionally haven't been addressed in this space.
To explore the nuances, the more subtle questions that allow visitors to get a much broader sense of what European paintings are and how we might consider them today.
